insights.headline: 28 readers jumped 2+ engagement points since Q3 — the water quality investigation is the common thread
insights.detail: The metrics engine flagged 28 readers whose engagement scores jumped significantly (2+ points) since the Q3 2025 baseline. All 28 had page views on your water quality investigation series. The series generated 123 page views on Part 1 and 108 on Part 2, making it your most-read content by a wide margin. Of these 28 jumpers, 10 became first-time donors — moving from non-donor to minor-donor status during the same period.
insights.action: Your investigative series created a measurable pipeline from casual reader to power user to donor. Plan a follow-up: 'Six Months Later: Lead Pipe Replacement Stalls' is already published but hasn't been promoted to this cohort. Send a targeted newsletter to these 28 readers linking back to the series and the new follow-up piece.
dataPoints: 28 readers jumped 2+ engagement points (computed from previousEngagementScore vs current)
All 28 jumpers read water quality articles (page view actions linked to water_quality content)
10 of 28 jumpers became first-time donors (previousDonorStatus: non_donor → current: minor_donor)
Water quality Part 1: 123 views, Part 2: 108 views — top 2 content by views

insights.headline: 22 readers in ZIP 63101 declined from avg 3.8 to 1.2 — all shared interest in school redistricting
insights.detail: The churn cluster analysis detected 22 readers in ZIP 63101 whose engagement dropped significantly. Their average previous score was 3.8; it's now 1.2. The shared topics among this cluster are school_redistricting, education, and local_government. Their last recorded content engagement was the redistricting articles ('Board Delays Vote After Backlash' and 'Redistricting Plan Draws Protests'). You published two redistricting articles (Aug-Sep 2025) and then stopped. These readers left when the coverage did.
insights.action: The redistricting vote still hasn't happened. Assign follow-up coverage and send a re-engagement email to the 63101 cluster referencing the original story. Of the 84 total readers in 63101, only these 22 churned — the others who had broader interests stayed. This is a recoverable audience if you restart the coverage.
dataPoints: 22-reader churn cluster in ZIP 63101 (computed: 3+ declining readers sharing ZIP and topics)
Avg engagement: 3.8 → 1.2 (from previousEngagementScore to engagementScore)
Shared topics: school_redistricting, education, local_government (40%+ overlap threshold)
Last content: redistricting articles published Aug-Sep 2025, no follow-up since

insights.headline: Education is your #2 topic by audience interest (123 readers) but has only 1 recent article
insights.detail: The topic interest map shows 123 readers list education as an interest — second only to local_government (204 readers). But the content analysis shows only 1 education article published since October 2025 ('Teacher Shortage Crisis'). Meanwhile, local_government has 13 recent articles. The mismatch is stark. Survey data reinforces this: 14 of 25 respondents mentioned education when asked what topics to cover more, and 15 of 25 mentioned education-related themes in 'what's missing.'
insights.action: This is your most clear-cut coverage gap. 123 interested readers, high survey demand, but almost no recent content. Establish at minimum a monthly education roundup. The redistricting story is the obvious starting point (it also addresses the 63101 churn cluster). This could be your highest-ROI editorial investment.
dataPoints: Education: 123 interested readers, 1 recent article (topic interest map)
Local_government: 204 interested readers, 13 recent articles (for comparison)
Survey: 14/25 respondents want more education coverage ('What topics?' question)
Survey: 15/25 mention education in 'What's missing?' — top theme by count

insights.headline: 7 survey respondents explicitly asked for more neighborhood-level reporting
insights.detail: In the 'What's missing?' survey question, 'neighborhood_coverage' was the second-most-common theme (7 mentions after education at 15). Respondents wrote things like 'More coverage of individual neighborhoods,' 'Need more coverage of North St. Louis,' and 'Coverage of my neighborhood (63115).' This aligns with the geographic data showing 95 readers in ZIP 63115 at avg engagement 3.6 — the largest reader cluster — but a relatively low donor conversion rate of 4%.
insights.action: The demand for neighborhood reporting is real and measurable. Pilot a 63115-focused newsletter or column — you have 95 readers there already engaging above average with housing and local government content. Measure whether dedicated neighborhood coverage moves the needle on engagement and donor conversion in that ZIP.
dataPoints: 7/25 survey respondents asked for neighborhood-level reporting
ZIP 63115: 95 readers, avg engagement 3.6, 4 donors (4% conversion)
63115 top topics: local_government, housing, health, crime, development
Specific respondent mentions of '63115' and 'North St. Louis'

insights.headline: Finance newsletters get 8-11 opens vs. 49-69 for other topics — and finance readers are churning
insights.detail: Newsletter performance data shows a stark split: finance/business newsletters (Dec 15, Jan 12, Jan 26) averaged 10 opens, while local government, housing, and environment newsletters averaged 56 opens. A second churn cluster (3 readers in 63116) shares finance/business interests. Finance content also underperforms in articles: 'Local Business Roundup Q4' and 'Q1' rank in the bottom tier by views. Meanwhile, education has 123 interested readers but finance has far fewer, yet finance gets equal newsletter space.
insights.action: Stop sending standalone finance newsletters — they're hurting your overall open rate metrics. Bundle finance news into your local government newsletter (your best-performing topic). Reallocate the editorial energy to education or neighborhood coverage where demand is proven.
dataPoints: Finance newsletters: 8-11 opens (Dec 15: 11, Jan 12: 10, Jan 26: 8)
Non-finance newsletters: 42-69 opens (Jan 5: 64, Feb 2: 61, Feb 23: 64)
3-reader churn cluster in 63116 with shared finance/business interests
Business Roundup articles rank bottom tier by total views

insights.headline: 31 total readers declined significantly — beyond the two main clusters, 6 are scattered
insights.detail: The metrics engine flagged 31 readers with significant engagement declines (2+ points or crossed from high to low). 22 are the 63101/redistricting cluster, 3 are the 63116/finance cluster, and 6 are scattered across other ZIPs with no shared pattern. These 6 may represent natural churn. Combined with 114 readers at score 1 currently (vs. 89 previously), the overall engagement trend shows a net increase in disengaged readers.
insights.action: For the scattered 6, there's no clear content intervention. Focus re-engagement effort on the two identified clusters where there's a clear cause (stopped coverage) and a clear fix (restart it). Monitor overall score-1 count as a health metric — if it keeps growing, it signals a broader retention problem.
dataPoints: 31 readers declined significantly (temporal comparison)
22 in 63101 cluster + 3 in 63116 cluster + 6 scattered
Score 1 readers: 89 (previous) → 114 (current), net +25
Score 4-5 readers: 197 (previous) → 203 (current), net +6 — growth is slower at the top

insights.headline: 151 highly-engaged readers haven't donated — that's your conversion pipeline
insights.detail: Of 203 readers at engagement 4-5, 151 are non-donors. The donor funnel shows 21 new donors this period, and the content-before-donation analysis reveals the pattern: 15 of those new donors had read the water quality Part 1, and 11 had read Part 2. The water quality investigation is your proven donor conversion trigger. Your current donor base is 54 (46 minor + 8 major), meaning the 151 high-engagement non-donors represent a 3x potential expansion.
insights.action: Don't send these 151 people a generic donation ask. Segment them by content interest and send targeted appeals tied to the journalism they already love. For the ~100 who read investigative/water quality content: 'Your support funds investigations like the water quality series.' For others: match the appeal to their topic interests. Time it with your next high-impact story.
dataPoints: 151 readers at engagement 4-5 with non_donor status (computed from engagement + donor fields)
21 new donors this period (previousDonorStatus: non_donor → current: minor/major_donor)
15 new donors read water quality Part 1 before donating (content-before-donation analysis)
Current donor base: 54 (46 minor + 8 major)

insights.headline: Water quality investigation is your proven donor engine — 15 of 21 new donors read it
insights.detail: The content-before-donation analysis is unambiguous: 15 of 21 new donors read 'Investigation: What's Really in St. Louis Drinking Water?' before their first donation. 11 also read Part 2. No other content comes close — the next highest is the redistricting articles at 5. Deep investigative reporting isn't just your best journalism, it's your most effective fundraising content.
insights.action: Fund your next investigation. The data makes the business case: investigative series are your highest-view content (123 + 108 views), your highest engagement-jump trigger (28 readers jumped), and your best donor converter (15 of 21 new donors). Build a 'Support Investigative Journalism' donation campaign timed to your next series launch.
dataPoints: 15/21 new donors read water quality Part 1 before donating
11/21 new donors read Part 2
Next closest: redistricting articles at 5 donors
Investigation series: 123 + 108 views (top content), 28 engagement jumpers, 10 new donors directly

insights.headline: ZIP 63115 is your largest reader cluster (95 readers, avg 3.6 engagement) with only 4% donor conversion
insights.detail: The geographic breakdown shows 63115 as your largest cluster with 95 readers at average engagement 3.6 — above the overall average. Their top interests are local_government, housing, health, crime, and development. Content match score is 100% (you publish on all their top topics), but donor conversion is just 4% (4 donors) compared to 12% in 63101 and 12% in 63103. The 63115 audience is engaged and reading — they're just not converting to donors.
insights.action: The 63115 audience needs a different approach. They're reading your content but don't feel invested enough to donate. Try dedicated neighborhood coverage — survey data shows demand for it. If 63115 readers see you covering *their* neighborhood specifically, the loyalty and conversion should follow. Track donor conversion in 63115 as your KPI.
dataPoints: 63115: 95 readers, avg engagement 3.6, 4 donors (4% rate)
63101: 84 readers, avg engagement 2.6, 10 donors (12% rate — even with churn cluster)
63103: 41 readers, avg engagement 3.0, 5 donors (12% rate)
63115 content match: 100% — they have content, they just need dedicated coverage

insights.headline: 63101 has your highest donor count (10) despite the worst average engagement (2.6) — the churn cluster is dragging the average
insights.detail: ZIP 63101 has 84 readers at just 2.6 average engagement — the lowest of your major clusters. But it also has 10 donors (12% rate). The reason: 22 recently-churned readers are pulling the average down hard. Removing the churned cohort, the remaining 62 readers in 63101 are actually a strong, engaged community with high donor participation. The churn cluster is a recent wound, not a chronic problem.
insights.action: Don't write off 63101. The 22 churned readers are addressable with education/redistricting coverage. If you recover even half, the average engagement bounces back. And the existing donor base there proves 63101 readers convert when served — this is a neighborhood worth investing in.
dataPoints: 63101: 84 readers, 2.6 avg engagement, 10 donors (12% conversion)
22 churned readers in 63101 with avg score 1.2 dragging down the average
Remaining 62 readers in 63101 would avg ~3.2 engagement without churn cohort
63101's 10 donors = tied for highest donor count among all ZIPs
